subj-group.subject: Research Briefs: Clinical Trial Brief Reports

title-group.article-title: Neoadjuvant BMS-813160, Nivolumab, Gemcitabine, and Nab-Paclitaxel for Patients with Pancreatic Cancer

article-meta.aff: 1Division of Medical Oncology, Department of Internal Medicine, Washington University, St. Louis, Missouri.
2Division of Public Health Sciences, Department of Surgery, Washington University, St. Louis, Missouri.

author-notes.corresp: * Corresponding Author: Kian-Huat Lim, Division of Oncology, Washington University in St Louis, 660 S. Euclid Avenue, Saint Louis, MO 63110. E-mail: kian-huat.lim@wustl.edu

permissions.copyright-statement: ©2025 The Authors; Published by the American Association for Cancer Research

license.license-p: This open access article is distributed under the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-ND 4.0) license.

sec.title: Purpose:
sec.p: Targeting tumor-associated macrophages through C–C chemokine receptor type 2 (CCR) in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) improves the efficacy of chemotherapy and restores T-cell immunity in preclinical models.

sec.title: Patients and Methods:
sec.p: We conducted a phase I/II single-institution study (NCT03496662) combining chemotherapy gemcitabine and nab-paclitaxel (GnP), CCR2/5 inhibitor BMS-813160, and nivolumab for four 28-day cycles for patients with borderline resectable (BR) or locally advanced (LA) PDAC. The recommended phase II dose of BMS-813160 was established in the 3 + 3 design. Primary endpoints were safety and objective response rate (ORR). Secondary endpoints included resection rate, median progression-free survival, and median overall survival.

sec.title: Results:
sec.p: Eight patients were treated with GnP alone (control arm), and 32 patients (29 response evaluable) were treated at the recommended phase II dose. One instance each of grade 3 diarrhea and QTc prolongation occurred which were atrributed solely to BMS-813160. After four cycles of study treatment (N = 22), the ORR was 42% and 20% among patients with BR and LA PDAC, respectively, compared with 0% of control patients. A total of 83.3% of patients with BR and 20% of patients with LA PDAC who completed study treatment underwent surgical resection. For intent-to-treat analyses, patients with BR PDAC had a median progression-free survival and median overall survival of 11.9 and 18.2 months, respectively, whereas patients with LA PDAC had 14.7 and 17 months, respectively. Biomarker analyses showed decreased intratumoral monocytes and macrophages and enhanced T-cell proliferation and effector gene expression.

sec.title: Conclusions:
sec.p: Neoadjuvant BMS-813160/nivolumab/GnP was well tolerated and seems to achieve comparable ORR and resectability with historical data; however, with prolonged PFS and OS in LA-PDAC patients, warranting a larger phase II study with a more efficacious CCR2-targeted therapeutic.

institution-wrap.institution: National Institues of Health/National Cancer Institute

boxed-text.p: Tumor-associated macrophages express C–C chemokine receptor type 2, which mediates their recruitment to the pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) tumor microenvironment, where they promote tumor cell survival and suppress T-cell–mediated immune responses. We conducted the first clinical trial combining standard chemotherapy with a C–C chemokine receptor type 2/5 inhibitor and anti-PD1 for patients with borderline resectable (BR) or locally advanced (LA) PDAC, for which neoadjuvant chemotherapy is commonly administered. Eight patients who received chemotherapy alone were included for the comparison of side effects. The quadruple regimen was well tolerated. Of patients who underwent at least one imaging scan post baseline (N = 29), this regimen yielded an objective response rate of 33% for patients with BR and 14% for patients with LA PDAC, respectively. Of patients who completed study treatment (N = 12 BR‐PDAC, N = 10 LA‐PDAC), 83% of BR‐PDAC and 20% of LA‐PDAC patients underwent resection. The progression-free survival and overall survival were prolonged in patients with LA PDAC with signs of on-target efficacy in tumor tissues and warrant further testing in a larger phase II study.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Approximately 30% of patients with pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) present with borderline resectable (BR) or locally advanced (LA) disease for which neoadjuvant chemotherapy is offered to increase the chance of an R0 surgical resection and survival. Using gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel (GnP) or FOLFIRINOX, approximately 42% to 72% of patients with BR tumors and 22% of patients with LA tumors achieve surgical resection (1, 2). The tumor microenvironment of PDAC is rife with immunosuppressive myeloid cells including inflammatory monocytes (IM) and tumor-associated macrophages (TAM; refs. 3–5), which stifle the effect of chemotherapy and T-cell immunity. IM expressing C–C chemokine receptor type 2 (CCR2) are chemotactically drawn from the bone marrow into PDAC tumors by C–C motif chemokine ligand 2 (CCL2), in which they evolve into TAM (5–7). Our institution completed a clinical trial combining a CCR2 inhibitor (CCR2i, PF-04136309) with FOLFIRINOX in patients with LA PDAC (NCT01413022). In that trial, 49% (16 of 33) patients achieved an objective tumor response (8), as compared with reported 13% with FOLFIRINOX alone for this patient population (9). Additionally, CCR2 blockade overcomes immune suppression to reinitiate antitumor responses via CD8+ T cells in mice (7). The regimen of FOLFIRINOX with PF-04136309 led to a 69% incidence of grade 3 to 4 neutropenia. Based on preclinical data using a GnP regimen and the high rate of neutropenia with FOLFIRINOX, we designed a phase I/II study using GnP in combination with an oral CCR2/CCR5 antagonist BMS-813160 and nivolumab for patients with BR and LA PDAC.

sec.title: Study design and participants
sec.p: This single-institution phase I/II open-label study (NCT03496662) was conducted at Washington University/Siteman Cancer Center in St. Louis, Missouri, under Institutional Review Board approval (No. 201806077) and conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki. This clinical trial was registered at Clinicaltrials.gov (NCT03496662) and conducted according to the CONSORT guidelines. All patients provided written informed consent. Sex as a biological variable is not considered, as PDAC affects all genders equally. Eligible patients were ≥18 years of age with histologically confirmed untreated BR or LA PDAC confirmed in the Washington University Pancreatic Multimodality Conference, with Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group ≤1 with normal bone marrow and organ function. Exclusion criteria included known or suspected autoimmune disease, human immunodeficiency virus positivity, gastrointestinal conditions that could impair absorption of BMS-813160, and QTcF >480 milliseconds.

sec.title: Procedures
sec.p: Eligible patients identified in the Washington University Pancreatic Multimodality Conference were treated in a dose-finding part (part A) in which they were randomized 1:1 to the control (GnP only) or investigational arm (BMS-813260, nivolumab, and GnP) until eight were enrolled in the control arm (Fig. 1). Decreasing dosage levels of oral BMS-813160 from 300 mg twice a day to 150 mg twice a day and 150 mg daily in a 3 + 3 design were used to identify the recommended phase II dose (RP2D) of BMS-813160 in combination with nivolumab 480 mg every 28 days with gemcitabine 1,000 mg/m2 and nab-paclitaxel 125 mg/m2 on days 1, 8, and 15 of a 28-day cycle (Table 1). Following identification of the RP2D, additional patients were enrolled into dose expansion (part B) until up to 30 patients were treated at the RP2D for at least one cycle. All patients underwent pretreatment biopsy and biopsy after two cycles. Patients with stable disease or partial response continued treatment and came off study after four total cycles (i.e., discontinued BMS-813160 and nivolumab but continued GnP if on the experimental arm). Patients who were deemed resectable after four cycles underwent resection, whereas those who were not resectable received either stereotactic body radiotherapy or next chemotherapy [continued GnP or transitioned to modifed (m) FOLFIRINOX] per treating physician discretion. Adverse events were monitored continuously while on study.

caption.p: Enrollment and clinical results of the study. A, CONSORT diagram showing enrollment, treatment, and analyses of study patients. B, Swimmer plot depicting treatment course and time on study for all patients with PDAC enrolled in this study. C, Waterfall plot showing changes of target lesion sum (best response) for all 37 response-evaluable study patients enrolled in each cohort based on RECIST 1.1 criteria. Kaplan–Meier curves depicting the PFS and OS for ITT patients (red-dotted line) and patients who completed four cycles of study treatment (red solid line) on investigational arm and all patients on control arm (black line) with (D) BR or (E) LA PDAC. ARDS, acute respiratory distress syndrome; SBRT, stereotactic body radiotherapy; tx, treatment.

sec.title: Outcomes
sec.p: The primary objectives were to determine the safety and objective response rate (ORR) of BMS-813160 and nivolumab in combination with GnP. Secondary objectives included the resectability rate after four cycles of treatment on the study regimen, as well as their progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS).

sec.title: Toxicities and safety
sec.p: Toxicity was graded according to the Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events v5.0. Dose-limiting toxicity was defined based on the incidence, duration, and grade of AE during the first cycle (28 days) of treatment in part A of the investigational arm. Treatment was continued for a total of four cycles or until disease progression, death, or withdrawal of consent.

sec.title: Response assessments
sec.p: Restaging scans were conducted after two and four cycles of treatment. Treatment response was defined by the international criteria proposed by the revised RECIST v1.1 criteria. Resectability was determined at the Washington University Pancreatic Multimodality Conference.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Clinical characteristics were summarized using descriptive statistics. OS was defined as the months from the date of treatment start [cycle 1 day 1 (C1D1)] to death. Alive patients were censored at the last follow-up. PFS was defined as the months from the date of treatment start (C1D1) to progression or death, whichever occurred first. Alive patients without progression were censored at the last follow-up. Survival probabilities were calculated using the Kaplan–Meier method and differences between two groups were determined by log-rank tests. All statistical tests were two-sided using an α = 0.05 level of significance. SAS version 9.4 was used to perform all statistical analyses.

sec.title: Single-cell RNA sequencing data generation and analysis
sec.p: Biopsies were manually minced and digested in 1× Hank’s Balanced Salt Solution containing 2 mg/mL Collagenase A (Roche) and 1× DNase I (Sigma) for 30 minutes at 37°C with stirring. Digestion was quenched with 5 mL FBS (Atlanta Biologicals), filtered through a 45-µm filter, pelleted by centrifugation (500 × g for 5 minutes), and resuspended in FACS Buffer [1% BSA solution in Dulbecco's Phosphate Buffered Saline (DPBS)]. Following tissue digestion, cells from each sample were encapsulated into droplets and libraries using the BD Rhapsody Single-Cell Capture and cDNA Synthesis Analysis System (BD Biosciences). The generated libraries were sequenced by a NovaSeq 6000 sequencing system (Illumina) to an average of 50,000 mean reads per cell and aligned and analyzed as previously described (10, 11). FASTQ files from each sample were processed and aligned with BD Rhapsody WTA Analysis Pipeline (Seven Bridges) using the reference genome GRCh38-PhiX-gencodev29.tar.gz to generate a feature barcode matrix. The filtered feature barcode matrices were loaded into Seurat as Seurat objects (Seurat v4). For each Seurat object, genes expressed in less than three cells and cells that expressed less than 1,000 or more than 6,000 genes were excluded. Cells with greater than 15% mitochondrial RNA content were also excluded. SCTransform was used on each individual sample to normalize and scale the expression matrix against the sequence depths and percentages of mitochondrial and ribosomal genes (12). Variable features were calculated for every sample in the dataset independently and ranked based on the number of samples they were independently identified (SelectIntegrationFeatures). The top 3,000 variable features were then used for principal component analysis (PCA; RunPCA). The calculated PCA embedding of each cell was then used as an input for the soft k-means clustering algorithm. Briefly, through iteration, the algorithm designated the cluster-specific centroids and cell-specific correction factors corresponding to batch effects. The correction factors were used to assign cells into clusters until the assignment was stable (RunHarmony). The first 20 PCA components were then used to refine the edge weights between any two cells based on Jaccard similarity (FindNeighbors) and were used to cluster cells through FindClusters. To characterize clusters, the FindAllMarkers function with log fold threshold = 0.25 and minimum 0.25-fold difference and MAST test were used to identify signatures unique to each cluster. Myeloid and lymphoid populations were then isolated for further downstream analysis based on their expression of CSF1R, C1QA, C1QB, FCGR3A, CD14, and CSF3R for myeloid cells and CD3E, CD8A, CD4, FOXP3, GZMA, and GZMB for lymphoid cells. Differentially expressed genes between pre- and posttreatment groups were calculated for each cluster with min.pct of 0.1 and logfc.threshold of 0.01 and MAST test (FindMarkers). Then, the differentially expressed gene lists from each dataset were filtered with P < 0.05 and ranked based on fold change. These ranked gene sets were fed into gene set enrichment analysis to test for Gene Oncology terms, Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes Pathways, Reactome, and Molecular Signatures Database gene sets with FDR <0.05 in ClusterProfiler (13).

sec.title: Flow-cytometry analysis
sec.p: Pre- and posttreatment blood samples and endoscopic ultrasound-guided biopsy tissues were obtained for biomarker studies. Peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMC) were isolated from blood using a Ficoll gradient and cryopreserved. For blood analysis, 32 paired PBMC samples from pretreatment and end of cycle 2 were analyzed for monocytes by flow cytometry. Analysis was performed using FlowJo Software v10. For tumor tissues, endoscopic ultrasound-guided biopsies were manually minced and digested in 1× Hank’s Balanced Salt Solution containing 2 mg/mL Collagenase A (Roche) and 1× DNase I (Sigma) for 30 minutes at 37°C with stirring. Digestion was quenched with 5-mL FBS (Atlanta Biologicals), filtered through a 45-µm filter, pelleted by centrifugation (500 × g for 5 minutes), and resuspended in flow-cytometry buffer (1% BSA solution in DPBS). Fresh single-cell suspensions were analyzed for myeloid cells by flow cytometry using FlowJo Software v10. Antibodies used for flow cytometry are provided in Supplementary Table S1.

sec.title: Data availability
sec.p: The RNA sequencing data generated in this study are available at the Gene Expression Omnibus under GSE288533. All other data are available upon request from the corresponding author.

sec.title: Enrollment
sec.p: Between September 21, 2018, and May 31, 2021, 53 patients with BR or LA PDAC were screened and 40 were eligible. In the part A dose-finding phase, patients were randomized 1:1 to the investigational (BMS-813160, nivolumab, and GnP) or control (GnP) arm until eight were enrolled into the control arm (Fig. 1A). The purpose of having a control arm was to allow comparison of the safety profile with the quadruple regimen. For the first seven patients treated on the investigational arm with BMS-813160 300 mg twice a day, no dose-limiting toxicity was observed, and subsequently 25 more patients were treated at this dosage in part B. Of all 32 patients treated on the investigational arm, 14 were female, 29 were White, and the median age was 69 years (range, 48–81). In the control arm, four were female, five were White, and the median age was 71 years (range, 64–78). Patients in both arms had similar baseline carbohydrate antigen (CA)19-9 values (Table 1; Supplementary Table S2).

sec.title: Safety
sec.p: In the part A dose-finding phase, there were no grade 3 to 4 treatment-emergent adverse events (TEAE) attributed to BMS-813160, and thus the RP2D of BMS-813160 was determined to be 300 mg twice a day. In the part B dose-expansion phase, grade 3 to 4 TEAE included alanine aminotransferase elevation, diarrhea, QTc prolongation, fatigue, nausea, and neutropenia, but only grade 3 diarrhea and grade 3 QTc prolongation were attributed solely to BMS-813160, as the remaining grade 3 to 4 TEAE were also partially attributed to GnP and nivolumab. Notably, the dose-expansion cohort had a similar frequency of high-grade (grade 3–4) TEAE compared with the control arm including rates of neutropenia, fatigue, diarrhea, transaminase elevation, pneumonitis, and rash (Table 2). All eight (100%) patients on the control arm completed study treatment, whereas 22 of 32 (69%) patients on the investigational arm completed study treatment. COVID-19 outbreak occurred during part B dose expansion. Two patients (subject No. 24 and No. 26) on the investigational arm contracted COVID-19, without leukopenia, during study treatment and were admitted to the intensive care units and later died from secondary bacterial complications. One (subject No. 44) voluntarily withdrew and received GnP after C1D1 of treatment. One patient (subject No. 6) voluntarily withdrew after cycle 1. Three patients (subject No. 30, 52, and 53) were removed prior to cycle 4 for unrelated TEAEs that precluded further treatment on study. One patient (subject No. 41) voluntarily withdrew prior to cycle 3. One patient (subject No. 28) was taken off the study because of noncompliance with taking BMS-813160. One patient (subject No. 48) died suddenly during the off week of cycle 1 when he was found unresponsive in his car while planning to drive home from work. After a thorough discussion with the treating oncologist, medical monitor, and the patient’s family, the cause of death was deemed indeterminate.

fn.p: Abbreviations: Alk. phos, alkaline phosphatase; ALT, alanine aminotransferase; AST, aspartate aminotransferase.
Attributed to BMS-813160a, nivolumabb, gemcitabinec, or nab-paclitaxeld.

sec.title: Efficacy
sec.p: The median duration of follow-up for all treated patients (N = 40) was 19 months (Fig. 1B). Of 32 patients treated on the investigational arm, three were deemed not evaluable, as subjects No. 6 (BR PDAC) and No. 44 (LA PDAC) withdrew before completion of cycle 1, and subject No. 48 (BR PDAC) suffered sudden death before cycle 2. Of the 32 intention-to-treat patients—including Ten who did not complete the study treatment—seven (22%) had a partial response: five of 17 (29%) with BR tumors and two of 15 (13%) with LA tumors, compared with zero of eight (0%) in the control arm (Fig. 1C). No patient on either arm had a complete response or disease progression during the four cycles of study treatment. Patients in the investigational arm had an average 70.3% best reduction in CA19-9 (range, 37–94), compared with an average 70.9% best reduction (range, 43–93) in the control arm. For patients who completed four cycles of study treatment (N = 22), 10 of 12 patients (83.3%) with BR PDAC and two of 10 patients (20.0%) with LA PDAC underwent surgical resection, compared with five of six patients (83%) with BR PDAC and zero of two (0%) with LA PDAC on the control arm. For intent-to-treat (ITT) patients, patients with BR PDAC (N = 17) had a median PFS (mPFS) and median OS (mOS) of 11.9 and 18.2 months, respectively (Fig. 1D); patients with LA PDAC (N = 15) had an mPFS and mOS of 14.7 and 17 months, respectively (Fig. 1E). In the ITT population, 11 of 17 patients (65%) with BR PDAC underwent resection and 9 of 17 (53%) had an R0 resection; for patients with LA PDAC, 2 of 15 (13%) underwent resection, all of whom (2/15) had an R0 resection. The patients with BR PDAC who did not undergo surgical resection did not do so because vascular involvement was too extensive and progressed after four cycles of protocol treatment or died due to complications of COVID-19 (N = 1). For patients with BR PDAC, there was no statistically significant difference in mPFS and mOS between control and investigational arms (Fig. 1D), whereas for patients with LA PDAC, the investigational arm patients had longer mPFS and mOS (Fig. 1E).

sec.title: Correlative studies
sec.p: Pre- and posttreatment (end of cycle 2) peripheral blood and tumor biopsies were collected to assess immune changes. Analysis of PBMC by flow cytometry surprisingly showed no change in circulating CD45+CD3/CD19/CD20/CD56(−) CD11b+CSF1R+CD14+CCR2+ IMs (Supplementary Fig. S1A). To determine whether there were differences in PDAC-infiltrating myeloid cells, we analyzed 38 pre- and posttreatment biopsy tissues, representing 16 patients on the investigational arm and eight on the control GnP arm. Flow-cytometry analysis of these samples showed decreased CD45+CD11b+CD14+CSF1R+CCR2+CD33+ IM/monocytic myeloid-derived suppressor cells and CD45+CD11b+MHCII+CD14midCD33(−)CD15(−) TAM in posttreatment samples from patients on the investigational arm (Fig. 2A). We also saw an increase in the neutrophil-to-TAM ratio in posttreatment tissues, suggesting potential on-target myeloid compensation (Fig. 2B). No changes in myeloid cells were observed in PDAC tissues from patients in the control arm (Fig. 2A). To explore changes in T-cell responses, we obtained PDAC tissue from patients in the investigational arm, representing four patients pretreatment and six patients posttreatment, and the immune cells were subjected to single-cell RNA sequencing (Fig. 2C). Following Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection and clustering of cells, we identified major myeloid and lymphoid cell types based on marker gene expression (Supplementary Fig. S1B). Quantification of cells in pre- and posttreatment samples showed increases in CD8+ T cells and proliferating T-cell clusters (Fig. 2D; Supplementary Fig. S1C). Gene expression testing and gene set enrichment analysis showed changes in CD8+ and CD4+ T cells that indicated enhanced T-cell effector function, including upregulation of TNF, IFNα/γ, and several T-cell receptor pathways in posttreatment CD8+ T cells (Fig. 2E). Furthermore, at the gene level, posttreatment CD8+ T cells showed upregulation of key effector genes such as IL1B, LTF, CD7, GZMA, and GZMB (Supplementary Fig. S1D–S1F). Taken together, these data suggest that we observed decreased myeloid infiltration and increased T-cell activation/effector function in posttreatment tumor tissue from patients in the experimental arm.

caption.p: Correlative studies of pre- and posttreatment tumor samples. A, Analysis of patient tumor tissue biopsy samples at pretreatment (pre) and the end of cycle 2 (post). Tumor biopsy tissues were analyzed by flow cytometry for CD45+CD11b+CD14+CSF1R+CCR2+CD33+ IM/monocytic myeloid-derived suppressor cells (Mo-MDSC) and CD45+CD11b+MHCII+CD14midCD33(−)CD15(−) TAM and CD45+CD11b+CD14(−)CD15(+) neutrophils/granulocytic myeloid-derived suppressor cells (G-MDSC). B, Analysis of neutrophil-to-TAM ratios using data from (A). The percent of CD45+ cells is depicted as mean ± SEM. n.s.-denoted groups are not statistically different. C and D, Uniform Manifold Approximation Projection (UMAP) plot showing different subsets of CD45+ immune cells categorized based on marker gene expression from all pre- and posttreatment tumor samples using single-cell RNA sequencing. E, Gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) showing regulated signature changes in CD8+ and CD4+ T-cell clusters (from D) in postttreatment tumor samples. NES, normalized enrichment score.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: To date, successful surgical resection remains the only way to cure patients with PDAC (14). For patients with BR or LA PDAC, neoadjuvant chemotherapies such as GnP and FOLFIRINOX are almost always recommended. Based on multiple studies including the TAPS study, A021501, and a meta-analysis from The Ohio State University, the resection rate of BR PDAC and LA PDAC is approximately 40% to 70% and 20%, respectively. In a case series reported by MD Anderson Cancer Center (MDACC), which included 680 patients with treatment-naïve BR PDAC (N = 267, 39.3%) and LA PDAC (N = 413, 60.7%), neoadjuvant chemotherapy resulted in a final resection rate of 24.1% for patients with BR PDAC and 9% for those with LA PDAC. It is noteworthy that in that study, 17.7% and 34.1% of patients did not complete neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX or GnP, respectively, which affected the subsequent resection and OS (9). In the phase II LAPACT study, which enrolled 106 patients with LA PDAC subsequently treated with GnP, the ORR was 33.6% (15). In another phase II study (NEOLAP-AIO-PAK-0113), which enrolled 168 patients with LA PDAC with 130 randomized and treated with either GnP alone or GnP followed by FOLFIRINOX, GnP treatment yielded a 22% ORR, a 35.9% conversion rate to surgery, mPFS of 7.7 months, and mOS of 18.5 months, all of which were not statistically different from the GnP followed by FOFLIRINOX arm (16).

Although our previous study combining FOLFIRINOX with PF-04136309 showed encouraging preliminary clinical and correlative results, to minimize the risk of neutropenia, and based on preclinical work done using GnP, here we chose to combine GnP with a CCR2/5 inhibitor and nivolumab. We show that this quadruple regimen did not incur further toxicities compared with eight patients who were treated with GnP alone. For patients who completed the study treatment (N = 22), the ORR for BR PDAC and LA PDAC was 42% and 20.0% respectively, compared with zero of eight (0%) in the control arm. The resection rate of the ITT population in the investigational arm was 65% for BR PDAC and 13% for LA PDAC, compared with 83% and 0%, respectively, in the control arm. The resection rate in the present study is similar to that reported in the literature. Compared with the phase II LAPACT study, which reported an mPFS of 7.7 and mOS of 18.15 months for patients with LA-PDAC, our ITT patients with LA PDAC had an mPFS of 14.7 months, which seems to be numerically better. However, our mOS for the ITT cohorts were 18.2 and 19.2 months, respectively, which were similar to the LAPACT study.

Although the quadruple regimen may have promising efficacy, there are limitations. First, the relatively small number of patients treated in both arms limits the generalization of our conclusions. Second, patients received only four cycles of study treatment before being referred for surgery or radiation, which may have limited its potential efficacy. Third, despite observing favorable cell pathway changes and reduction of intratumoral TAM or IM, paradoxically, we did not observe changes in IM in peripheral blood from these same patients. The IM are mobilized from the bone marrow and chemotactically drawn by CCL2 into PDAC tumors (17). Small-molecule CCR2 inhibitors such as BMS-813160 and PF-0413630 reversibly block CCR2 receptors on IM, and in theory, “sequester” in the bone marrow (7, 8, 18), but this effect is also accompanied by compensatory mechanisms such as upregulation of circulating CCL2 levels and enrichment of CXCR2-expressing neutrophils (19). Therefore, IM in the bone marrow may still be mobilized into the blood circulation when effective serum concentrations of BMS-813160 drop, which is likely to occur when we collected the posttreatment tumor tissues on the day when BMS-813160 was held. Therefore, we hypothesize that a CCR2-targeting agent that can durably suppress or eliminate the IM may achieve a better therapeutic response.

In summary, our study shows the safety and potential efficacy of CCR2i/CCR5 inhibitor plus anti-PD1 and standard GnP for the neoadjuvant treatment of PDAC. Based on our results, suppressing the TAM and IM through targeting the CCR2–CCL2 axis requires the development of more efficacious therapeutics and careful consideration of compensatory mechanisms.

caption.p: Supplementary Figure 1. Data supporting correlative analyses.
Supplementary Table 1. Antibodies used in flow cytometry.
Supplementary Table 2. Representativeness of study participants.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: During the conduct of this study, J. Liu, D.G. DeNardo, and K.-H. Lim were supported by the National Cancer Institute P50CA196510 and P50CA272213. We thank Bristol Myers Squibb for providing BMS-813160 and nivolumab and for their input on the manuscript.

fn.p: Note: Supplementary data for this article are available at Clinical Cancer Research Online (http://clincancerres.aacrjournals.org/).

sec.title: Authors’ Disclosures
sec.p: P.M. Grierson and K.-H. Lim report personal fees from Aclaris Therapeutics outside the submitted work, as well as a patent for MK2 inhibitor for pancreatic cancer issued and with royalties paid from Washington University in St Louis and a one-time payment from AstraZeneca in January 2024 (advisory board). L. Ratner reports grants from NIH. P. Oppelt reports other support from Merck and HiFiBiO outside the submitted work. A. Frith reports personal fees from Gilead, Novartis, Hologic, Lumanity, IQVIA, Horizon, Curio Science, Amerisource, Dava Oncology, Cardinal Health, OncLive, and Targeted Oncology outside the submitted work. K.S. Pedersen reports personal fees from Bristol Myers Squibb outside the submitted work. J.M. Baer reports grants from NIH during the conduct of the study, as well as grants from NIH outside the submitted work. J. Liu reports grants from NIH/NCI and grants from NIH during the conduct of the study. D.G. DeNardo reports grants from NIH/NCI and grants from BJC Foundation during the conduct of the study. K.-H. Lim reports nonfinancial support and other support from Bristol Myers Squibb during the conduct of the study. No disclosures were reported by the other authors.

sec.title: Authors’ Contributions
sec.p: P.M. Grierson: Resources, data curation, formal analysis, investigation, writing–original draft. C. Wolf: Data curation, writing–original draft. R. Suresh: Investigation. A. Spann: Investigation, writing–review and editing. N. Boice: Data curation, investigation. A. Brown: Data curation, investigation. J.M. Baer: Software, visualization. E.G. Butka: Software, formal analysis. F. Ahmad: Software, formal analysis. Y. Xu: Formal analysis, visualization. J. Liu: Formal analysis, investigation, visualization, methodology, writing–original draft. D.G. DeNardo: Conceptualization, software, formal analysis, validation, visualization. K.-H. Lim: Conceptualization, resources, data curation, formal analysis, supervision, writing–original draft, writing–review and editing.
